body:
## PR Reviewer Guide 🔍 #### (Review updated until commit https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/commit/270fc8d0f9a0c1330a14ab6eef236f250ab44557)
⏱️ Estimated effort to review: 4 🔵🔵🔵🔵⚪
🧪 PR contains tests
🔒 No security concerns identified
⚡ Key issues to review

Code Smell
The `StrictFormatter` class does not handle the case where `args` is not empty and `kwargs` is empty. It only checks if `args` is not empty, but does not check if `kwargs` is empty. Possible Bug
The `Prompt` class does not validate if the `input_variables` are actually used in the `template`. It only checks if the `template` is valid with dummy inputs. Test Coverage
The tests for `StrictFormatter` do not cover all possible scenarios, such as testing with an empty `template` or with a `template` that has multiple placeholders.
author: Arjun7A

author: sjwhitmore

body: After examining the structure of the chained prompts (e.g. self ask with search & ReAct & Natbot), I think a less opinionated approach to the structure of the examples (e.g. a list of examples as strings rather than a list of examples as dicts with specific key-value pairs) is more likely to generalize across all prompts. I think the value of a list of examples is 1) readability 2) dynamically omitting an example if my input to the prompt is too long. I don't think we need to be too opinionated about the key/value pairs in the examples as we're already trusting the user to structure their prompt templates correctly... we don't want to make it too onerous to fit every example into the key/value input_variables + output_suffix paradigm. I'll push an update which simply accepts examples as a list.
author: sjwhitmore

body:
Pushed an example with the ReAct template... not quite sure how best to work in the example sizing into the Chain class.
Option 1: Add a num_examples argument to the __call__() method which takes a splice of the list of examples and uses that to initialize prompt in the _run() method. This feels like it muddies up the interface a bit, and also requires knowledge of the examples themselves and how long they are.
Option 2: Add a dynamic_prompt_sizing=True argument to the config, which detects if a prompt edges towards the token limit (or receives an over token limit error from an LLM) and automatically cuts an example. This feels like it might be a bit too magical / hiding too much config from the user.
Option 3: Explicitly let the user choose to use the example splicing in any further classes they define, and don't integrate it into the classes here.
Option 4: ?? Am I missing anything here?
author: sjwhitmore

body:
yeah i like the change to go to whole examples. we can also do both! expose different constructor methods depending on the use case

related to your last point around incorporating dynamic examples, what do you think about just having a different prompt class? this prompt class could be like `DynamicPrompt`, and could take in init a list of examples, and then the dynamic removal of examples (if necc) comes into play when you call `.format`?
author: hwchase17

body:
potentially theres two separate ideas/prs here:

- make it easy to specify a prompt from examples (what currently is here)
- make a separate dynamic prompt class (in follow up pr)
author: hwchase17

body:
makes sense! I like the idea of adding a DynamicPrompt in a separate PR.
I forgot to add the 3rd reason for having an explicit list of examples (which originally motivated my comment about this over text haha) -- making it easier to generate test data more easily.

> This somewhat breaks the paradigm introduced in LLM base class as the stop sequence isn't a list, and should rightfully be introduced at the time of initialization of the class, along with the other kwargs that depend on its presence (e.g. remove_end_sequence, etc.) curious if you'd want to refactor LLM base class to take out stop as a specific named kwarg?

is `end_sequence` the same as stop_sequence[0] basically?
author: hwchase17

body: Goal here is to dynamically choose examples best suited to a particular query. We accomplish this by running the examples through the embedding class when initializing the prompt, and storing them in a vector store backend. When the prompt format method is called, the class selects the most semantically similar examples to use in the template.

![Screen Shot 2022-12-04 at 7 38 45 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11986836/205544242-31597830-f4c3-4863-a2b1-fe371542bb96.png)
author: hwchase17

body:
I've been thinking more about naming for things like PythonREPL or BashProcess (and soon, Crawler etc)

- `primitives` is possible but to me connotes something even more basic, like integers or strings or something
- `instruments` isn't bad, but has multiple meanings
- `tools` is probably the best but is a good name for the intermediate chains
- `utilities` is also pretty good, sometimes means helper function imo
- `utensils` to me sounds like a tool that is very low level, a small hand-held tool

I think utilities or utensils and kind of lean toward the latter. anyway just some thoughts!
author: coyotespike

body:
> so im down to check this in as is because i think its great, but one thing id like to think about before checking this in - how general could we make this? eg what is the most general prompt/chain that i could have that would work with this?

In the most general case, I think this verification becomes an agent, not a chain? You probably want to have an agent list its assumptions and then verify them with its tools, e.g. go to Wikipedia to look up a list of mammals.

This actually looks a lot like a parallel version of self-ask?

@hwchase17
author: andychenmagrathea-06e0a82f-34fc-48ca

body:
> > so im down to check this in as is because i think its great, but one thing id like to think about before checking this in - how general could we make this? eg what is the most general prompt/chain that i could have that would work with this?
> 
> In the most general case, I think this verification becomes an agent, not a chain? You probably want to have an agent list its assumptions and then verify them with its tools, e.g. go to Wikipedia to look up a list of mammals.
> 
> This actually looks a lot like a parallel version of self-ask?
> 
> @hwchase17

oh i like that idea. in that framing the checkers are the tools then right? so what part of this chain is that checker/tool? not the whole part, because it also contains the question. maybe the next two parts at the end?
author: hwchase17

body:
Right so I guess there's two options:

1) If we turn LLMCheckerChain into an agent, the verification of each individual assumption is routed to the appropriate tool by the agent. I can see this as potentially being more robust, at the cost of increased compute (especially if you allow the agent to call itself; self-ask doesn't have as much of a problem because there's no fanout)

2) On the other hand, I do see benefits of keeping things as-is and having a fast-running verification chain as just a tool — seems like a cheap step to throw in if an agent is unsure of an answer.

Maybe we do both? #1 used when compute / latency isn't as much an issue, #2 more widely used to make things a bit more correct?
author: andychenmagrathea-06e0a82f-34fc-48ca

body:
> Right so I guess there's two options:
> 
> 1. If we turn LLMCheckerChain into an agent, the verification of each individual assumption is routed to the appropriate tool by the agent. I can see this as potentially being more robust, at the cost of increased compute (especially if you allow the agent to call itself; self-ask doesn't have as much of a problem because there's no fanout)
> 2. On the other hand, I do see benefits of keeping things as-is and having a fast-running verification chain as just a tool — seems like a cheap step to throw in if an agent is unsure of an answer.
> 
> Maybe we do both? #1 used when compute / latency isn't as much an issue, #2 more widely used to make things a bit more correct?

what would (1) look like exactly? would we change the prompt of the agent, to tell it to answer the question and then use a tool to fact check? what would that tool look like? would htat tool basically be the prompts here?

is there a common prereq for both (1) and (2)? namely the tool/chain that does the verfication? and in (2) its just a simple sequence of original LLM -> verificaton chain, and in (1) its a tool the agent has access to?

author: varunnair18

body:
> see the comment, i dont love that we remove the single input choice for lllm_chain....
> 
> what if we just kept one class but do something like
> 
> ```
> def run(self, *args, **kwargs) -> str:
>       if len(args) == 1 #check if single variable and use that
>      check **kwargs and use that if multi
> ```

Yeah I proposed something like this in the notion doc I sent you. The exact signature you wrote won't work though, it would have to return `Dict[str, str]` since if using `**kwargs`, you could expect multiple outputs. This def works but I think the main problems with using `*args` and `**kwargs` here are that 1) they are used mutually exclusively -- you need some error checking code that validates the input at runtime, 3) for single input chains, you have to unpack the output 4) if using one class, you still have the problem of `apply` function for single input chains expected a `List[Dict[str, Any]]`, which broke the convention of keeping `input` and `output` keys abstracted away from the user.

I think the benefit of having two separate base interfaces is that it's harder to get things wrong and send funky input to the `run` function. It also removes the need for excess validation logic (no need for `SimpleSequentialChain` to validate that all chains are single in/out, just make all the chains `SingleVariableChains`)

If there is a big quality of life improvement from not having to specify the input variables in `run` for the Multi chain (like `LLMChain`) when there is only one input key, we can add something like this to `MultiVariableChain`, we can probably use the python `multipledispatch` library to overload `run` (either directly in `Chain` or keeping the two classes).

So many options! maybe we should discuss in person

body:
hmmm so i think i see what you are getting at, first, some clarifying questions/assumptions:

1) are the chains you always want to join together going to be LLM chains? given that you are call .prompt it seems yes

If so, i think it is totally valid to want a "chain of prompts" basically where you just append to the previous prompt. but i would do it in a constructor, rather than its own chain. what i mean by that is, i would:

1. add a `extend_prompt` method to the base prompt class, which takes in a template and input vairables and extends the previous prompt (this is because there are different types of prompt classes, not all have a `.template`
2. add a constructor method to sequential chain that takes in an initial prompt and then follow up strings and creates a chain, eg

```
def from_prompt_chain(cls, llm: LLM, initial_prompt: PromptTemplate, extensions: List[Tuple[str, List[str]]]):
      chains = [LLMChain(llm=llm, prompt=initial_prompt]
      current_prompt = initial_prompt
      for template, input_vars in extensions:
            current_prompt = current_prompt.extend_prompt(template, input_vars)
            chains.append(current_prompt)
      return cls(chains)
```

does that make sense? happy to help with this - a few other folks had brought up similar things so i do think it would be good to get in!
author: hwchase17

body:
@hwchase17 
> are the chains you always want to join together going to be LLM chains? given that you are call .prompt it seems yes
Interesting question. 
I guess it's LLMChain, but those could be embedded in other chain (Like LLMRequestsChain) ?

For exemple, if you do:
```python
template = """Between >>> and <<< are the raw search result text from google.
Extract the answer to the question '{query}' or say "not found" if the information is not contained.
Use the format
Extracted:<answer or "not found">
>>> {requests_result} <<<
Extracted:"""

template_follow_up = """
What is the total size of their population ?
"""
```
I would want to get the LLMRequestsChain prompt & response (via LLMRequestsChain.llm_chain)
(A) Do you think we should try to handle this use case or too hard for now?

> add a extend_prompt method to the base prompt class, which takes in a template and input vairables and extends the previous prompt (this is because there are different types of prompt classes, not all have a .template

Agree with you its imperfect. I don't like to recreate the prompt here. Maybe we should store prompt inside the LLMResult and use that as the base to access the prompt given. (B) Do you think there is an issue with this approach?

> add a constructor method to sequential chain that takes in an initial prompt and then follow up strings and creates a chain, eg

The issue I see here is that we couldn't specify the different call output names (in my example responseA & responseB), that we may want to get too. (I do)
author: BenderV

author: hwchase17

body:
@hwchase17 hey... try to dig up a bit this old PR...

I'm still struggling to see what's best, and how does it fit in the global philosophy of the library. I think we should probably be cautious about this one. 🤔 

Regarding your PR https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/384, it's missing some features comparing to the current solution
1. ability to retrieve intermediary outputs
2. ability to use intermediary input
3. ability to use different chain configuration 
4. ability to use something different from LLMChain

I have added an example with the point 1, 2 & 3 (but I guess you will understand the 4 based on the 3)
https://gist.github.com/BenderV/15d9a58d88318034c6ef27db5219bba7

To put some context, I had the need for the project https://generator.universaldata.io, where I had multiple steps, and I wanted an easy way to join them.
Here an extract of the code in question https://gist.github.com/BenderV/1ab57ad175c0c1385ae7c4fad1d9224b

You might notice that I didn't use SequentailChainWithPreviousContext for the last call, simply because I need to add some custom rules/processing on one of the intermediary inputs... (which let me to think that we might think bigger ?)

body:
With a few tweaks this can be made generic enough to implement agent/agentexecutor on top of it (which may not be the goal here). The tweaks would be
- make it optionally recursive (ie. after the follow up chain returns ask the decision chain again, with optional depth limit)
- add an optional summary chain (which in some implementations can be the same as the decision chain) that runs after the follow up chain selected, before this returns to the user

Actually, disregard this - I see that the code I was using locally and copied in would require additional dependences (sqlalchemy_utils) to be added to the project. I'll resubmit a more general PR that just allows for specifying a custom cache schema.

Anyone who's reading this because you had issues using Postgres with the cache, try using some variation of my code but be sure to include
```
from sqlalchemy import Sequence
from sqlalchemy_utils.types.ts_vector import TSVectorType
```
to get the required classes to load.

body:
@hwchase17  Yes, I think the issue right now is that SQLAlchemyCache is hard coded to load the "FullLLMCache" class (schema) so it can't be overridden by the user. Perhaps just doing something similar to my changes to the SQLAlchemyCache class itself:

https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/469/commits/fb558ed777fbd62e66dcf7bf9ceb809c8d0c1ead#diff-c3df233f3f011b5f20418c51538041dd60bc613da5123df0ebdcdc1637147ab9R69-R100

would allow users to create their own schema classes (like I did) and load them, without having to also make their own copy of the SQLAlchemyCache (which is what I've done to get around this, for now, and didn't realize I had added some imports to my local copy before submitting the PR).

I suspect there's probably a "better way" of doing this as well, since you'd want your custom SQLAlchemy schema to match the same fields/names (response, prompt, llm, idx) required by the SQLAlchemyCache class, but I'm not sure if there is a way to define that

body:
For instance, I'm using it like so:

```python
class FinishReasonChain(LLMChain):
    prefer_finish_on: Optional[str] = None
    
    def apply(self, input_list: List[Dict[str, Any]]) -> List[Dict[str, str]]:
        """Utilize the LLM generate method for speed gains."""
        response = self.generate(input_list)
        outputs = []
        for generation in response.generations:
            # Get the text of the top generated string.
            response_str = generation[0].text
            
            # If `prefer_finish_on_stop` then try to pick that
            if self.prefer_finish_on:
                responses = sorted(
                    generation,
                    key=lambda g: g.finish_reason == self.prefer_finish_on,
                    reverse=True
                )
                
                response_str = responses[0].text
            
            if self.verbose:
                langchain.logger.log_llm_response(response_str)
            outputs.append({self.output_key: response_str})
        return outputs

```
author: creatorrr

body:
IMHO, prefer outputs with `finish_reson=='stop'` should be part of the default implementation. OpenAI itself recommends it. Do you think it makes sense to patch the `OpenAIChain` with that. Something like what I mentioned earlier:

```python
class FinishReasonChain(LLMChain):
    prefer_finish_on: Optional[str] = None
    
    def apply(self, input_list: List[Dict[str, Any]]) -> List[Dict[str, str]]:
        """Utilize the LLM generate method for speed gains."""
        response = self.generate(input_list)
        outputs = []
        for generation in response.generations:
            # Get the text of the top generated string.
            response_str = generation[0].text
            
            # If `prefer_finish_on_stop` then try to pick that
            if self.prefer_finish_on:
                responses = sorted(
                    generation,
                    key=lambda g: g.finish_reason == self.prefer_finish_on,
                    reverse=True
                )
                
                response_str = responses[0].text
            
            if self.verbose:
                langchain.logger.log_llm_response(response_str)
            outputs.append({self.output_key: response_str})
        return outputs
```
author: creatorrr

body: @hwchase17 I am running into an issue with the link re-writes. Since they are rewritten from `.rst`, `.md`, and `.ipynb`, they are unable to be found by `linkcheck`. I put a brute force example in the `conf.py` of how we _could_ solve this if necessary, but I would much rather have a clean method. Unfortunately, I'm not too well-versed on Sphinx to know if there's a "right" way to do this or not. Any ideas you have would be appreciated.
author: HunterGerlach

body:
> > I don't mind doing it to free you up for other more important tasks if you just want to do a few so I can see an example or two (e.g. `.md` and `.ipynb`).
> 
> i _think_ (not 100%, need to spend some time to be sure) that ":doc:`foo <utils/how_to_guides>`:" should work?

I was unable to get it to work with `make linkcheck` - I think it's because it's checking the pre-compiled links _before_ their extensions change and it gets confused.

I switched to using a dedicated linkchecker instead.

I have removed all broken links, so running this from now on should prevent any broken links from getting into `master`.
author: HunterGerlach

author: jameswang14

body:
I don't really understand when this would happen. Aren't we forcing each document to have a text key when we build the index? I refer to these lines in `/langchain/vectorstores/pinecone.py`:

```
for j, line in enumerate(lines_batch):
      metadata[j][text_key] = line
to_upsert = zip(ids_batch, embeds, metadata)
```

I guess what would make more sense is to raise an error if a document does not have a text key in the metadata, what do you think?
author: fpingham

body:
> I don't really understand when this would happen. Aren't we forcing each document to have a text key when we build the index? I refer to these lines in `/langchain/vectorstores/pinecone.py`:
> 
> ```
> for j, line in enumerate(lines_batch):
>       metadata[j][text_key] = line
> to_upsert = zip(ids_batch, embeds, metadata)
> ```
> 
> I guess what would make more sense is to raise an error if a document does not have a text key in the metadata, what do you think?

The issue isn't when there isn't a text key in the metadata, it's when it's the wrong text key. If you submit two documents with two different text keys and then query pinecone with something that returns both documents this method will throw a KeyError because it isn't expecting a different text key. Since this method essentially just filters out results by text key, I don't think it makes sense to log a warning or an error as it is intended behavior (from what I understand).
author: bernie-g

body:
I understand, thanks for the clarification. I think the intended usage is that all documents have the same `text_key` which is the one used in the retrieval (there should be no `KeyError`).

However, with the current configuration a `KeyError` is possible given that the user can send a `text_key` in the constructor and another in the `from_texts` method. This enables potential mismatches between the two text keys.

I think a neat solution for this would be for `from_texts` to use `self._text_key` to build the metadata. This way there would be no possibility of mismatch. This would make sense only if the intended usage is the one I mentioned above, am I correct in that @hwchase17? Or do we want to let users use different text keys for building and for querying?
author: fpingham
